title: Driving Exchange Interaction in Spin Qubits with Quasi-Zero Pulses
authors: Julian D. Teske, Remy L. Delva, Shobhan Kulshreshtha, Yuval Baum, Florian Luthi, Fahd A. Mohiyaddin, Rostyslav Savytskyy, Thomas Watson, Pranav S. Mundada
abstract: The implementation of high-fidelity quantum gates for spin qubits requires accurate control of exchange interactions between electrons confined in quantum dots, but pulse distortions can limit this control accuracy. Although linear-dynamical distortions can be compensated for by appropriately convolving the control signal, determining the necessary convolution requires detailed knowledge of the distortion's transfer function, and therefore the calibration of numerous parameters. Alternatively, control pulses can be designed to have a net-zero time integral canceling out linear-dynamical pulse distortions. We generalize net-zero pulse designs to quasi-zero pulses allowing net-positive but reduced time integrals. Using these pulse designs, we systematically develop complete gate sets for exchange-only qubits, and study the resulting tradeoffs between pulse duration, fidelity, and the required number of tunable parameters, both in simulation and experiment. We benchmark the optimized gate pulses on Intel's Tunnel Falls six-dot device and show they achieve fidelities similar to those obtained with a full filtering approach, with identical pulse durations and fewer tuning parameters. This reduction in complexity opens the door to fast and easily automated calibration schemes compatible with large-scale commercial quantum devices.
summary: This paper tackles the challenge of controlling spin qubit exchange interactions accurately despite imperfections in control pulses. The authors introduce and validate 'quasi-zero' pulse designs as an efficient alternative to complex filtering methods. This approach significantly reduces the number of tunable parameters needed for high-fidelity gate operations, making quantum device calibration faster and more scalable.
main_problem: The primary challenge is achieving high-fidelity quantum gates for spin qubits by accurately controlling exchange interactions while mitigating systematic errors introduced by pulse distortions.
main_result: Quasi-zero pulses provide a low-complexity method to achieve gate fidelities comparable to highly optimized, multi-parameter filtering techniques, requiring fewer tuning parameters.
method: The authors designed and benchmarked quasi-zero pulses—a generalization of net-zero pulses—to cancel out linear-dynamical pulse distortions during the evolution of exchange-only qubits.
model_or_system: The work focuses on semiconductor spin qubits confined in quantum dots, specifically tested on Intel's Tunnel Falls six-dot device. The system dynamics are governed by an effective Hamiltonian involving tunable exchange interactions ($J_{i}(v_i)$).
key_observables: Gate fidelity (measured via randomized benchmarking), pulse duration, and the curvature of Rabi fringes.
important_parameters: The amplitude ratio ($\gamma$) for quasi-zero pulses is critical; optimal performance was found by balancing noise sources like hyperfine and charge noise.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes that control signal distortions are linear-dynamical. Furthermore, the negative pulse segments are assumed to have a negligible effect on the exchange interaction due to its exponential dependence on voltage.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the structure of atomic barrier pulses and compare fringe patterns, showing systematic shifts in reference pulses versus vanishing shifts with net-zero/quasi-zero pulses. Table I summarizes fidelity metrics for various pulse settings.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by first identifying the need to correct pulse distortions; it then introduces and analyzes net-zero and quasi-zero pulse designs, systematically benchmarking these against reference methods using randomized benchmarking to quantify performance gains in terms of both fidelity and parameter count.
why_interesting: This work directly addresses practical limitations in solid-state quantum computing hardware by providing a robust, low-complexity pulse shaping technique that simplifies the calibration overhead required for large-scale devices.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Illustration of an atomic barrier pulse as a function of time. A rectangular exchange pulse would correspond to an amplitude ratio of γ = 0, while a net-zero pulse would correspond to γ = tpos/tneg. Quasi-zero pulses correspond to other values of 0 < γ < tpos/tneg. In the following, we assume tpos = tneg. Inset: picture of the Tunnel Falls device adopted from Ref. [3].

FIG. 2. Pulse perturbation of net-zero and reference pulses. (a) Experiment description: The qubit is initialized, Np rep- etitions of N(ψ) pulses are applied and the qubit is read out. Each N(ψ) pulse is tuned to rotate the qubit an angle ψ around the n-axis. (b, c) Measured fringe pattern of the ref- erence pulse and the net-zero pulse. We plot the excitation probability as a function of the rotation angle ψ and number of pulse repetitions Np which are incremented in multiples of 2. The teal circles mark the center of the fringe pattern for different repetition counts and black dashed lines are included as guide to the eye to spot the curvature of the fringes.

FIG. 3. Comparison of fringe curvature induced by reference, net-zero and quasi-zero pulses. The plot displays the simulated ground-state fidelity F|0⟩following the multi-pulse sequence outlined in Fig. 2 (a). The teal dots are the peaks along the central fringe and the black dashed line marks the calibrated amplitude to implement the N(π/2) gate. We observe that the net-zero pulse bends the curvature of the central fringe and over-corrects the curvature on the considered time-scale. The optimal amplitude relation γ of a quasi-zero pulse is found by maximizing the alignment of the teal dots along the central fringe to a straight line.
